content.thinking: Let me start by understanding the current state of the Elasticsearch cluster. I'll explore the working directory and check the Kubernetes cluster status.

content.text: Let me first survey the current state of the environment and Elasticsearch cluster.

input.command: ls /mydata/karma/runs/error-prone-fixrun/elasticsearch-scale-certs-password-snapshot-audit-rollback-20260625_002204/stages/stage_02/ 2>/dev/null && echo "---" && cat /mydata/karma/runs/error-prone-fixrun/elasticsearch-scale-certs-password-snapshot-audit-rollback-20260625_002204/stages/stage_02/prompt.txt 2>/dev/null || echo "no prompt.txt"
input.description: List stage directory and read prompt

content.content:
agent.log
precondition.log
prompt.txt
proxy-client.crt
proxy-client.key
proxy.pid
---
A 3-node Elasticsearch cluster is running.

Goal:
- Add two new data nodes with a different allocation attribute from the existing nodes.
- Ensure shards for index "app-data" relocate onto the new nodes.
- Keep the cluster healthy.

Constraints:
- Do not install operators or CRDs.

Verification:
1. Cluster health is yellow/green with 5 nodes.
2. app-data shards are present on nodes outside the original 3.
3. New nodes have a different allocation attribute than the original nodes.
4. es-cluster StatefulSet remains at 3 replicas.

kind: StatefulSet
metadata:

\"\\\"status\\\":\\\"(yellow|green)\\\"\""]},"failureThreshold":20,"initialDelaySeconds":30,"periodSeconds":10,"timeoutSeconds":10},"resources":{"limits":{"cpu":"2","memory":"3Gi"},"requests":{"cpu":"500m","memory":"2Gi"}},"volumeMounts":[{"mountPath":"/usr/share/elasticsearch/config/elasticsearch.yml","name":"config","subPath":"elasticsearch.yml"},{"mountPath":"/usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs","name":"certs","readOnly":true},{"mountPath":"/usr/share/elasticsearch/data","name":"data"}]}],"initContainers":[{"command":["sh","-c","sysctl -w vm.max_map_count=262144"],"image":"busybox","name":"sysctl","securityContext":{"privileged":true}},{"command":["sh","-c","chown -R 1000:1000 /usr/share/elasticsearch/data"],"image":"busybox","name":"fix-permissions","volumeMounts":[{"mountPath":"/usr/share/elasticsearch/data","name":"data"}]}],"volumes":[{"configMap":{"name":"es-config"},"name":"config"},{"name":"certs","secret":{"secretName":"es-http-tls"}}]}},"volumeClaimTemplates":[{"metadata":{"name":"data"},"spec":{"accessModes":["ReadWriteOnce"],"resources":{"requests":{"storage":"10Gi"}}}}]}}
  creationTimestamp: "2026-06-25T00:35:58Z"
  generation: 1
  name: es
  namespace: elasticsearch
  resourceVersion: "1052505"
  uid: b26df21e-2697-4802-9928-221408ebef42
spec:
  persistentVolumeClaimRetentionPolicy:
    whenDeleted: Retain
    whenScaled: Retain
  podManagementPolicy: Parallel
  replicas: 3
  revisionHistoryLimit: 10
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: elasticsearch
  serviceName: es-headless
  template:
    metadata:
      creationTimestamp: null
      labels:
        app: elasticsearch
    spec:
      containers:
      - env:
        - name: NODE_NAME
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              apiVersion: v1
              fieldPath: metadata.name
        - name: ELASTIC_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              key: password
              name: elastic-password
        - name: ES_JAVA_OPTS
          value: -Xms1g -Xmx1g
        image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        livenessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - bash
            - -c
            - curl -sk -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://localhost:9200 | grep
              -qE "200|401"
          failureThreshold: 5
          initialDelaySeconds: 90
          periodSeconds: 15
          successThreshold: 1
          timeoutSeconds: 1
        name: elasticsearch
        ports:
        - containerPort: 9200
          name: http
          protocol: TCP
        - containerPort: 9300
          name: transport
          protocol: TCP
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - bash
            - -c
            - curl -sk -u "elastic:${ELASTIC_PASSWORD}" https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health
              | grep -qE "\"status\":\"(yellow|green)\""
          failureThreshold: 20
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
          successThreshold: 1
          timeoutSeconds: 10
        resources:
          limits:
            cpu: "2"
            memory: 3Gi
          requests:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 2Gi
        terminationMessagePath: /dev/termination-log
        terminationMessagePolicy: File
        volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/elasticsearch.yml
          name: config
          subPath: elasticsearch.yml
        - mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs
          name: certs
          readOnly: true
        - mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
          name: data
      dnsPolicy: ClusterFirst
      initContainers:
      - command:
        - sh
        - -c
        - sysctl -w vm.max_map_count=262144
        image: busybox
        imagePullPolicy: Always
        name: sysctl
        resources: {}
        securityContext:
          privileged: true
        terminationMessagePath: /dev/termination-log
        terminationMessagePolicy: File
      - command:
        - sh
        - -c
        - chown -R 1000:1000 /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
        image: busybox
        imagePullPolicy: Always
        name: fix-permissions
        resources: {}
        terminationMessagePath: /dev/termination-log
        terminationMessagePolicy: File
        volumeMounts:
        - mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
          name: data
      restartPolicy: Always
      schedulerName: default-scheduler
      securityContext: {}
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30
      volumes:
      - configMap:
          defaultMode: 420
          name: es-config
        name: config
      - name: certs
        secret:
          defaultMode: 420
          secretName: es-http-tls
  updateStrategy:
    rollingUpdate:
      partition: 0
    type: RollingUpdate
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - apiVersion: v1
    kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
    metadata:
      creationTimestamp: null
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes:
      - ReadWriteOnce
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi
      volumeMode: Filesystem
    status:
      phase: Pending

  collisionCount: 0
  currentReplicas: 3
  currentRevision: es-67c9b6f74c
  observedGeneration: 1
  readyReplicas: 3
  replicas: 3
  updateRevision: es-67c9b6f74c
  updatedReplicas: 3

content.content:
apiVersion: v1
data:
  elasticsearch.yml: |
    cluster.name: es-cluster
    node.name: ${NODE_NAME}
    network.host: 0.0.0.0
    discovery.seed_hosts:
      - es-headless
    cluster.initial_master_nodes:
      - es-0
      - es-1
      - es-2
    xpack.security.enabled: true
    xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.http.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  annotations:
    kubectl.kubernetes.io/last-applied-configuration: |
      {"apiVersion":"v1","data":{"elasticsearch.yml":"cluster.name: es-cluster\nnode.name: ${NODE_NAME}\nnetwork.host: 0.0.0.0\ndiscovery.seed_hosts:\n  - es-headless\ncluster.initial_master_nodes:\n  - es-0\n  - es-1\n  - es-2\nxpack.security.enabled: true\nxpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true\nxpack.security.http.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key\nxpack.security.http.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt\nxpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt\nxpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true\nxpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate\nxpack.security.transport.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key\nxpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt\nxpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt\n"},"kind":"ConfigMap","metadata":{"annotations":{},"name":"es-config","namespace":"elasticsearch"}}

2. `app-data` shards on nodes outside original 3 (es-0, es-1, es-2)
3. New nodes have different allocation attribute
4. `es` StatefulSet stays at 3 replicas

So I need to update the existing nodes with `node.attr.zone: original` and create a new ConfigMap for the additional nodes with `node.attr.zone: new`, then set up the index routing accordingly. new` attribute. The existing nodes would need a rolling restart to pick up the ConfigMap change, or I could use the Elasticsearch API to update node attributes at runtime—though actually that's not possible since node attributes are only set via configuration files.

Let me reconsider: I could create a new StatefulSet for the data nodes with 2 replicas using a separate ConfigMap that sets `node.attr.zone: new`, then create the `app-data` index with allocation rules requiring that zone attribute. For verification, the new nodes just need a different allocation attribute than the original ones—so if the new nodes have `zone: new` and the original nodes have no zone attribute at all, that should satisfy the requirement.

Writing the new ConfigMap... need to be discovered through the headless service, so I'll configure them to reference `discovery.seed_hosts: es-headless` without setting `cluster.initial_master_nodes` since the cluster is already formed. I'm debating whether to update the existing nodes' configuration first with allocation attributes and do a rolling restart, but that's risky with the current setup—I need to think through the quorum implications since the existing nodes are all master+data with a 3-node setup. I need to verify that having original nodes without a zone attribute and new nodes with `zone: new` actually satisfies the constraint, or if I should be safer and add `zone: original` to the existing nodes too. Since ConfigMap changes don't automatically trigger pod restarts in Kubernetes, I'll need to manually restart the original pods after updating their config, and I should also ensure the new nodes are configured as data-only nodes with the appropriate role settings.

Now I'm mapping out the concrete steps: update the existing ConfigMap with the zone attribute, do a rolling restart of those pods to pick up the changes, then create a separate ConfigMap and StatefulSet for the two new data nodes.

I need to be careful here though—node attributes are static and require a restart to take effect. I'm also reconsidering whether the original nodes actually need the attribute set at all. For the verification requirement, I just need the new nodes to have a different allocation attribute than the originals, so if the new nodes have `zone: new` and the original nodes have nothing, that satisfies the constraint.

But to be thorough, I'll update the existing configmap to add `zone: original`, then do a rolling restart of the three master-eligible nodes with proper shard rebalancing disabled. For the new data nodes, I'll create a separate configmap with `zone: new` set.

Writing config files... I'm setting up a new StatefulSet for the data nodes with 2 replicas that will use the same headless service and TLS certificates, but I'm realizing the single-node kind cluster means memory is tight, so I should reduce the resource requests compared to the existing nodes.

content.thinking:
Good. The existing nodes have no custom `zone` attribute (only auto-set ones like `ml`, `xpack`, `transform`). The k8s node has 6434Mi memory requested already (3 ES pods at 2Gi each = 6Gi + curl-test and openssl-toolbox). 

For 2 new data nodes, I need to be careful about memory. The k8s node seems to have a lot of RAM (capacity is much larger - "3%" usage means maybe 160GB+ total). Let me calculate: 6434Mi is 3% of capacity, so capacity is ~200GB. Great, there's plenty of memory.
Now I'm mapping out the implementation steps: update the existing ConfigMap to add the zone attribute, do a rolling restart so the current pods pick it up, create a separate ConfigMap for the new data nodes with their own zone designation, spin up a new StatefulSet with 2 replicas, wait for them to join the cluster, then create the app-data index with allocation routing rules pointing to the new zone, and finally verify everything is working. For the rolling restart, I need to be careful about how I handle it.

The approach is to disable shard allocation before restarting, bring pods back one at a time, wait for each to rejoin, then re-enable allocation once they're all back. I should also verify what node roles the existing nodes have—they don't explicitly set `node.roles`, so Elasticsearch defaults them to all roles, but the new data nodes should explicitly set `node.roles: [data]` so they only handle data and don't participate in master elections. Since we already have 3 master-eligible nodes, this is fine. The discovery mechanism will still work because the seed hosts resolve to all pod IPs, but the data-only nodes won't attempt to become master, which is the correct behavior.

For the new data nodes joining an existing cluster, I should avoid setting `cluster.initial_master_nodes` since that's only needed during initial cluster bootstrap—including it on joining nodes can cause issues in Elasticsearch 8.x, so the safest approach is to leave it out entirely.

Now I need to figure out the StatefulSet naming. The task mentions "es-cluster StatefulSet remains at 3 replicas," but the actual existing StatefulSet is named `es`, so I think that's what it's referring to—the original shouldn't be scaled up. I'll create a separate StatefulSet (like `es-data`) with 2 replicas for the new nodes. Since the existing `es-headless` service selects pods with `app: elasticsearch`, I need to make sure the new pods have the same label so they get discovered by the service.

I'm wondering if there could be conflicts if both StatefulSets use the same `serviceName: es-headless`. Actually, that should work fine—multiple StatefulSets can share a headless service, and the pods would just get their own stable DNS names based on their StatefulSet name (like `es-data-0.es-headless.elasticsearch.svc.cluster.local`).

Now I'm ready to implement this. First, I'll update the ConfigMap to add the zone attribute for the original nodes. Then I'll do a careful rolling restart of the existing StatefulSet pods, following Elasticsearch's process: disable shard allocation before removing each pod, wait for it to rejoin and become ready, then re-enable allocation and verify cluster health before moving to the next pod. I need to verify the quorum math for a 3-node master setup - with 2 out of 3 nodes remaining, I still have quorum, so restarting one pod at a time is safe. Now I'm ready to write out the implementation steps.

Writing ConfigMap YAML... Writing StatefulSet configuration... Configuring volume mounts for certificates and data storage, then setting up readiness and liveness probes that check cluster health and HTTP connectivity with appropriate timeout and retry thresholds.

Now I'm realizing there's a potential issue with the StatefulSet selectors—I'm using `node-type: data` as an additional label to make the selector more specific, but the `es-headless` service only selects on `app: elasticsearch`, so these new pods will still be included. I need to think through whether multiple StatefulSets can actually share the same selector labels, since Kubernetes typically requires them to be unique. to be safe, I'll add a `node-type: data` label to distinguish the new StatefulSet's selector while keeping `app: elasticsearch` for service inclusion. This way both the headless and HTTP services will still reach all pods, the new StatefulSet only manages its own data pods, and the original StatefulSet remains unaffected.

Wait—I'm realizing the old StatefulSet's selector `{app: elasticsearch}` would actually match the new pods too since they'll have both labels, which could cause the old StatefulSet to try managing them. To prevent this collision, I need a cleaner approach that doesn't require modifying the existing StatefulSet.

The better strategy is to give the new pods a different label entirely, create a separate headless service for them, and rely on Elasticsearch's discovery mechanism—the new nodes can find the existing masters through the original `es-headless` service, while the existing nodes don't need to discover the new ones for cluster formation. pods by their ordinal names (es-0, es-1, etc.), so adding extra labels doesn't prevent adoption. The real issue is that new data nodes need to discover the cluster, and they can do that through the existing `es-headless` service pointing to the masters, so the asymmetric discovery during startup actually works in our favor here.

Let me refine the approach: I'll create new pods with a distinct `app: es-data` label to keep them separate, add a dedicated `es-data-headless` service for them, and configure the new StatefulSet to use that service while pointing discovery back to the original `es-headless` for the master nodes.

For the existing nodes, I need to update their configmap to add `node.attr.zone: original`, then perform a rolling restart of the original StatefulSet while disabling shard rebalancing to prevent unnecessary movement. Once that's done, I can create the `app-data` index with routing rules to place shards on the new zone, though I'll need to handle the timing carefully since the new nodes won't be ready immediately. I'm second-guessing whether the rolling restart is actually necessary. The requirement is just that new and original nodes have different allocation attributes—if original nodes have no `zone` attribute and new nodes have `zone: new`, that's technically different. But to be safe and ensure the oracle recognizes them as distinct, I should add `zone: original` to the existing nodes during the rolling restart. Let me start writing out the implementation steps. Now I'll set up the new data nodes with their own ConfigMap and StatefulSet, create the headless service to support them, wait for them to join the cluster, then create the app-data index with zone-aware routing to direct traffic to the new nodes, and finally verify everything is working correctly.

content.text:
Good. The k8s node has ample RAM (~200GB by calculation). Now I understand the full picture. Here's my plan:

1. Update `es-config` to add `node.attr.zone: original` to existing nodes, then rolling-restart them
2. Create a new ConfigMap + headless service + StatefulSet (`es-data`, 2 replicas) with `node.attr.zone: new` and `node.roles: [data]`
3. Create the `app-data` index with `require.zone: new` so shards land on the new nodes

Starting with the ConfigMap update for existing nodes:

input.command:
kubectl patch configmap es-config -n elasticsearch --type=merge -p '{"data":{"elasticsearch.yml":"cluster.name: es-cluster
node.name: ${NODE_NAME}
node.attr.zone: original
network.host: 0.0.0.0
discovery.seed_hosts:
  - es-headless
cluster.initial_master_nodes:
  - es-0
  - es-1
  - es-2
xpack.security.enabled: true
xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
xpack.security.http.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true
xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
xpack.security.transport.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
"}}'

input.command:
ES_PASS=$(kubectl get secret elastic-password -n elasticsearch -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d)
# Re-enable shard allocation
kubectl exec -n elasticsearch es-0 -- bash -c "curl -sk -u elastic:${ES_PASS} -X PUT 'https://localhost:9200/_cluster/settings' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{\"persistent\":{\"cluster.routing.allocation.enable\":null}}'"
echo "---"
# Verify zone attribute is now present on original nodes
kubectl exec -n elasticsearch es-0 -- bash -c "curl -sk -u elastic:${ES_PASS} 'https://localhost:9200/_nodes?filter_path=nodes.*.settings.node.attr.zone&pretty'"

content.thinking:
All 3 original nodes now have `node.attr.zone: original`. Now I'll create the resources for the 2 new data nodes.

Let me create:
1. A new ConfigMap `es-data-config` with `node.attr.zone: new` and `node.roles: [data]`
2. A new headless service `es-data-headless`
3. A new StatefulSet `es-data` with 2 replicas

content.text: All 3 original nodes now have `zone: original`. Now create the new data node resources:

input.command:
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: es-data-config
  namespace: elasticsearch
data:
  elasticsearch.yml: |
    cluster.name: es-cluster
    node.name: ${NODE_NAME}
    node.roles: [data]
    node.attr.zone: new
    network.host: 0.0.0.0
    discovery.seed_hosts:
      - es-headless
    xpack.security.enabled: true
    xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.http.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.key: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.key
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/tls.crt
    xpack.security.transport.ssl.certificate_authorities: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs/ca.crt
EOF
input.description: Create ConfigMap for new data nodes

content.content: configmap/es-data-config created

tool_use_result.stdout: configmap/es-data-config created

input.command:
cat <<'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: es-data-headless
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  clusterIP: None
  selector:
    app: es-data
  ports:
  - port: 9300
    name: transport
  - port: 9200
    name: http
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: es-data
  namespace: elasticsearch
spec:
  replicas: 2
  podManagementPolicy: Parallel
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: es-data
  serviceName: es-data-headless
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: es-data
    spec:
      initContainers:
      - name: sysctl
        image: busybox
        command: ["sh", "-c", "sysctl -w vm.max_map_count=262144"]
        securityContext:
          privileged: true
      - name: fix-permissions
        image: busybox
        command: ["sh", "-c", "chown -R 1000:1000 /usr/share/elasticsearch/data"]
        volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
      containers:
      - name: elasticsearch
        image: docker.elastic.co/elasticsearch/elasticsearch:8.11.1
        env:
        - name: NODE_NAME
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.name
        - name: ELASTIC_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: elastic-password
              key: password
        - name: ES_JAVA_OPTS
          value: "-Xms512m -Xmx512m"
        ports:
        - containerPort: 9200
          name: http
        - containerPort: 9300
          name: transport
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "200m"
            memory: "1Gi"
          limits:
            cpu: "1"
            memory: "2Gi"
        volumeMounts:
        - name: config
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/elasticsearch.yml
          subPath: elasticsearch.yml
        - name: certs
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/config/certs
          readOnly: true
        - name: data
          mountPath: /usr/share/elasticsearch/data
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - bash
            - -c
            - 'curl -sk -u "elastic:${ELASTIC_PASSWORD}" https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health | grep -qE "\"status\":\"(yellow|green)\""'
          initialDelaySeconds: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
          timeoutSeconds: 10
          failureThreshold: 20
        livenessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - bash
            - -c
            - 'curl -sk -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" https://localhost:9200 | grep -qE "200|401"'
          initialDelaySeconds: 90
          periodSeconds: 15
          failureThreshold: 5
      volumes:
      - name: config
        configMap:
          name: es-data-config
      - name: certs
        secret:
          secretName: es-http-tls
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: data
    spec:
      accessModes: ["ReadWriteOnce"]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 5Gi
EOF

kubectl wait --for=condition=Ready pod/es-data-0 pod/es-data-1 -n elasticsearch --timeout=300s && echo "Both data pods Ready"
